one_line_takeaway: Because MCP servers overwhelmingly cache or scope authorization at the server level instead of binding it to the invoking caller, 46.4% of 6,137 real-world MCP servers analyzed with the authors' CodeQL-based MCPAuthChecker are exploitable via 'caller identity confusion' -- letting an attacker-controlled agent silently inherit a legitimate user's prior authorization without any credential theft.
key_finding: Across 6,137 real-world MCP servers, 2,846 (46.4%) exhibited insecure authorization behavior (missing, cached, or reused across invocations). Insecure patterns were pervasive across all functional categories and star ranges rather than confined to immature projects (e.g., in the >1000-star bin, 65.2% of servers -- AuthNone 25.8% + AuthCache 9.1% + AuthRuntime 40.9% -- were still classified vulnerable). Developer Tools was both the largest category (by count) and among the most insecure (>50% insecure per the text; Table 7 shows 52.0% vulnerable for Developer Tools: 25.1% AuthNone + 4.1% AuthCache + 28.2% AuthRuntime out of the reported breakdown). Vulnerable-capability analysis (Figure 7) showed Developer Tools servers dominate every consequence category: 1,196 insecure servers enabling network/external communication, 748 enabling persistent data access, 398 enabling system-level execution, and 394 enabling physical/human-interface interaction -- far above any other category. Vulnerability was also highly author-concentrated: the top vulnerable author ('mcp-mirror') alone accounted for 58 vulnerable servers, and the top three authors by total server count were also the top three by vulnerable-server count, with roughly one-third of their servers vulnerable. Tool-extraction recall was 94.08% (Python) and 100.00% (JS/TS), with false-positive rates of 7.40% and 8.04% respectively; authorization-detection recall was 92.30% (Python) / 90.10% (JS/TS); capability-detection recall exceeded 94% on both languages. Analysis time scaled with codebase size (steep growth above 3,000 LOC), dominated by CodeQL dependency extraction rather than the security-analysis stage itself. In the manual disclosure study of 87 GitHub projects (>1K stars), 8 projects were found to have authentication-related issues permitting remote command execution; the team responsibly disclosed these (directly or via CVE coordination), with several maintainers confirming/acknowledging or already having patched the issue, and at least one fix independently verified by the authors post-disclosure.
core_contribution: The paper identifies and formalizes 'caller identity confusion' in MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers: because MCP servers frequently cannot distinguish which caller (agent/client) triggered a given tool invocation, a single authorization decision (e.g., an OAuth grant or cached credential established by one legitimate caller) can implicitly govern subsequent tool invocations issued by a different, potentially untrusted caller, without re-authentication. To detect this at scale, the authors build MCPAuthChecker, a static+dynamic analysis framework (built on CodeQL) that (1) resolves execution-trigger points for tool invocations despite heterogeneous, often dynamic tool-registration/dispatch patterns across MCP server implementations, (2) performs path-sensitive analysis from each tool entry to resource-affecting operations to determine whether authorization is enforced per-invocation, cached at server level (AuthCache), enforced only partially at runtime (AuthRuntime), or absent (AuthNone), and (3) applies selective dynamic validation via a unified mcp-remote proxy that observes real tool-call traffic (covering both SSE and stdio server modes) to resolve cases where static analysis alone cannot determine whether authorization state is actually enforced per-invocation at execution time. The authors then run MCPAuthChecker across 6,137 real-world MCP servers collected from MCPCollection and MCPZoo, and additionally responsibly disclose 8 vulnerabilities found via a manual review of 87 GitHub MCP-related projects with >1K stars.
threat_model: The authors state their threat model explicitly (Sec 3.2): (1) prior to any adversarial activity, a legitimate user has already interacted with the MCP server and completed authorization, so the server holds a valid, established authorization state; this does not guarantee exploitability, since servers with correctly caller-bound, per-invocation authorization reject the subsequent attack. (2) The attacker controls a separate agent or invocation source that can remotely issue tools/call requests to the already-authorized MCP server (e.g., via the protocol's remote-invocation support, such as npx mcp-remote http://IP:port/mcp). (3) The attacker does not control the MCP server process itself and does not obtain credentials or authorization material directly -- no credential theft or memory corruption is required; the attack exploits authorization-state reuse. The paper explicitly notes this is not a flaw in the MCP specification itself but an implementation consequence: MCP permits caching authorization state for efficiency but does not mandate that the state be scoped/attributed to the invoking caller.
limitations: The paper's own text surfaces several limitations rather than claiming full soundness: (1) tool-extraction and authorization-detection are not perfect -- measured false-negative rates up to 9.90% (JS/TS authorization detection) and false-positive rates up to 9.00% (Python capability detection), meaning the large-scale 46.4% vulnerability figure inherits some noise from imperfect ground-truth extraction. (2) The dynamic validation phase (Phase 3) is described as 'selective,' applied only when static analysis cannot resolve authorization enforcement, so its coverage is inherently partial and depends on the mcp-remote proxy correctly exercising realistic runtime behavior. (3) The dataset (6,137 servers from MCPCollection and MCPZoo) is explicitly noted by the authors to be concentrated around a small number of marketplaces (Smithery Registry, MCPMarket, Pulse MCP together contribute over two-thirds of servers), which the authors themselves flag as a potential source of non-representativeness/aggregation bias rather than a uniformly sampled ecosystem view. (4) The classification scheme (AuthNone/AuthCache/AuthRuntime vs. secure/caller-bound) depends on the analysis correctly attributing authorization semantics across highly heterogeneous, often dynamically-dispatched implementations, which the paper itself frames as a core technical challenge rather than a solved problem. (5) The excerpt read does not include the full Appendix A.1 (defense mechanisms) or the complete Section 6 attack case studies beyond the first (Agent-Originated RCE via kjozsa_git-mcp), so a full accounting of all four demonstrated real-world attack scenarios (system execution, data access, network, physical/UI) is not verified against text beyond what was extracted.

datasets_used: 6,137 MCP server projects (Python and JavaScript/TypeScript) sourced from MCPCollection and MCPZoo
100-project manually-validated sample (50 Python, 50 JS/TS) for tool-extraction/authorization/capability-detection accuracy, ground truth via dynamic source-code inspection
100 randomly sampled projects binned by LOC for efficiency/runtime measurement
87 GitHub open-source MCP-related projects with >1K stars, manually analyzed for disclosure case studies
quantitative_results: Overall vulnerability rate: 2,846 / 6,137 servers (46.4%) exhibit insecure authorization (AuthNone, AuthCache, or AuthRuntime)
Developer Tools category: 52.0% insecure (Table 7: 25.1% AuthNone + 4.1% AuthCache + 28.2% AuthRuntime; 48.0% secure)
>1000-star servers: 65.2% still vulnerable (25.8% AuthNone + 9.1% AuthCache + 40.9% AuthRuntime)

Vulnerable-server capability exposure (Figure 7): Developer Tools leads every category -- 1,196 servers enabling network/external communication, 748 enabling persistent data access, 398 enabling system-level execution, 394 enabling physical/human-interface interaction
Top vulnerable author ('mcp-mirror') accounts for 58 vulnerable MCP servers, out of 187 total servers by that author
Manual disclosure study: 8 of 87 (>1K-star) GitHub projects had authentication issues enabling remote command execution; responsibly disclosed via direct report or CVE coordination
baselines_compared: No prior automated tool is compared as a baseline; the paper positions itself as the first large-scale empirical study of this vulnerability class, and validates MCPAuthChecker's own components (tool extraction, authorization detection, capability detection) against manually-established ground truth rather than against a competing tool
